researchFindings.claim: Find two trees 6–8 feet apart and lash a sturdy horizontal ridgepole between them at about waist to shoulder height
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core frame construction step — ridgepole is the foundation of the lean-to

researchFindings.claim: If no trees are available, use forked sticks as upright support poles, lashed at the top, to support the ridgepole
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Backup frame method when trees are not available

researchFindings.claim: Lean long branches at a 45-degree angle against one side of the ridgepole to form the sloped roof frame; space them close together to support insulation
Layer natural materials (pine boughs, leafy branches, bark, leaves) over the angled poles starting from the bottom, overlapping each layer upward like shingles to shed rain
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Insulation layering method — shingling is the established technique for rain shedding

researchFindings.claim: In heavy snow conditions, rafter branches must be spaced very close together regardless of insulation material so the weight does not collapse the shelter
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Snow load warning — critical safety consideration

researchFindings.claim: Sleeping directly on the ground causes conductive heat loss; pile pine needles, leaves, soft bark, or other debris 4–6 inches thick on the floor as a thermal break
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Ground insulation — ground conduction is a leading cause of cold injury in shelters

researchFindings.claim: Orient the open face of the lean-to away from prevailing wind; optionally add branch windbreaks to the sides of the entrance to retain heat
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Wind orientation — critical for heat retention and rain protection

researchFindings.claim: A lean-to does not insulate well on its own; it requires a fire positioned on the open side to reflect heat into the shelter for overnight warmth in cold conditions
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Best Survival Shelter: A-Frame, Debris, Teepee, Lean-To?
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core limitation — sets correct expectations so users supplement with fire or better insulation

researchFindings.claim: Choose a flat, dry site elevated above low-lying flood zones; avoid positioning directly under dead branches or large limbs ('widow makers') that could fall
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Site selection safety — standard field guidance from BSA outdoor education

researchFindings.claim: Lash branches and cordage (paracord, vines, strips of bark, or shoelaces) at the ridgepole junction and at the top of upright poles to keep the structure stable
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Frame stability — lashing prevents ridgepole collapse under weather load

identifiedGaps.area: Exact insulation depth for reliable rain protection
identifiedGaps.impact: Sources agree to layer thickly but don't specify a minimum depth for pure rain shedding (debris hut guidance specifies 3 feet but that's for thermal insulation, not lean-to rain shedding)
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Guide should advise 'several inches' of layering, erring toward thickness without a hard number

recommendation: Write a concise action_card for building a natural-material lean-to frame (no tarp). Should cover site selection, frame construction using trees or forked sticks, rafter placement at 45°, shingled insulation layering bottom-to-top, floor insulation, and wind orientation. Include warning about lean-to's poor thermal retention requiring fire. Keep steps to 6–8 scannable items.
